flashcards.back: A data structure is a specialized format for organizing, processing, and storing data that enables efficient access and modification. It provides a way to manage large amounts of data effectively.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between primitive and non-primitive data structures?
flashcards.back: Primitive data structures are basic data types (int, float, char) provided by the language. Non-primitive data structures are user-defined structures that organize multiple data items (arrays, linked lists, trees).

flashcards.front: What are the two main categories of data structures?
flashcards.back: The two main categories are Linear Data Structures (where elements are arranged in sequence like arrays, linked lists) and Non-Linear Data Structures (where elements are not in sequence like trees, graphs).

flashcards.front: What is an Abstract Data Type (ADT)?
flashcards.back: An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a mathematical model that defines a data type by its behavior (operations) without specifying implementation details. It hides the internal representation from the user.

flashcards.front: What is the importance of time complexity in data structures?
flashcards.back: Time complexity measures the amount of time an algorithm takes to complete as a function of input size. It helps evaluate and compare the efficiency of different data structures for operations like insertion, deletion, and search.
Space complexity measures the amount of memory space required by an algorithm or data structure to solve a problem, including the space for input data and auxiliary space.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between data structures and algorithms?
flashcards.back: Data structures provide the mechanism to store and organize data, while algorithms provide the step-by-step procedures to manipulate that data. They work together - choosing the right data structure significantly affects algorithm efficiency.

A static data structure has a fixed size determined at compile time. Memory is allocated in contiguous memory locations. Examples include arrays. They offer fast access but limited flexibility in size.
A dynamic data structure can grow or shrink during runtime. Memory is allocated dynamically as needed. Examples include linked lists, trees. They offer flexibility but may have slower access times.

flashcards.front: What are the basic operations performed on data structures?
flashcards.back: The basic operations include: Insertion (adding elements), Deletion (removing elements), Traversal (accessing all elements), Searching (finding an element), Sorting (arranging elements in order), and Merging (combining two structures).

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case time complexity?
flashcards.back: Worst-case time complexity represents the maximum time taken by an algorithm for any input of size n. It provides a guarantee on performance and is denoted using Big O notation, e.g., O(n), O(log n).

Big O notation is a mathematical notation that describes the limiting behavior of a function when the argument tends towards infinity. In data structures, it expresses the upper bound of time or space complexity of an algorithm.
An array is a collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations. All elements must be of the same data type. It provides O(1) random access but O(n) insertion and deletion in worst case.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between linear and non-linear data structures?
flashcards.back: Linear data structures arrange elements in a sequential order where each element has a unique predecessor and successor (except first and last). Non-linear structures have elements connected in a hierarchical or network manner, allowing multiple relationships.

Asymptotic analysis is a method of describing the limiting behavior of a function when the argument tends towards infinity. In algorithm analysis, it describes the performance or complexity of an algorithm in terms of input size n.
